reasoning: Peer reference is a dense, cool-toned dungeon/stone tileset: greys, blacks, teal accents, very high feature density per tile (mechanisms, statues, debris, doors), hard-stepped shading with fine pixel articulation. Candidate is a warm green outdoor tile with scattered white rubble and golden-yellow flora — a completely inverted warmth/saturation axis. (1) Palette overlap: near-zero; peers are cool grey/teal, candidate is warm green/gold — surface type difference (grass vs stone) is acknowledged but the hue departure is so extreme it reads as a different game entirely. (2) Pixel density: candidate's feature count is ~30-40% of peers; large uniform color blocks dominate, very few distinct pixel-level details. (3) Shading technique: both use hard-stepped — only dimension that passes. (4) Warmth/saturation: peers are desaturated cool, candidate is highly saturated warm — direct inversion, not just a surface-type variation. (5) Hand-pixeled charm: peers show deliberate micro-detailing (cracked stone edges, individual tile joints, object silhouettes); candidate is coarse chunky blobs lacking that crafted quality. (6) Photo-realism contamination: none in either — both clean pixel art. (7) Overall feel: candidate reads as a different, simpler-art-budget game; placing it beside the peer dungeon tiles would cause an immediate visual break. Score 4 (fail).
